story.title: Dr. Huang and the Energy Roller Coaster
story.text:
Hi, I’m Dr. Mei-Ling Huang. I’m an engineer who designs roller coasters. My job is to make sure every ride is thrilling but also super safe. To do this, I study how energy works.

Think about the biggest hill on a coaster. A motor pulls the car all the way to the top. Up there, the car is full of stored energy. We call this potential energy. It’s like a stretched rubber band, waiting to go! A taller hill gives the coaster more of this stored energy.

Then, the car tips over the edge. Whoosh! As it races down, the stored energy transforms into moving energy. This is called kinetic energy. The more potential energy you start with, the more kinetic energy you get. That means a faster, more exciting ride at the bottom of the hill.

So, why does the coaster eventually stop? The secret is a force called friction. The wheels rub against the track, and this rubbing creates heat. This heat loss means some kinetic energy is leaving the coaster. Energy isn't destroyed, it just changes into heat. This is energy conservation. My job is to plan for this energy change so the coaster always has enough speed to make it back to the station.
think_abouts: What would happen to the coaster's speed if the first hill was much shorter?
Besides the track, where else might friction happen on a roller coaster?
story.go_deeper: How would a very windy day change the coaster's energy? (Hint: Think about air resistance.)

margin_defs.definition: Stored energy, like at the top of a hill.
Moving energy, like going down the hill fast.

check_prompts: What kind of energy turns into kinetic energy as the coaster goes down a hill?

vocabulary.definition: Stored energy an object has because of its position or height — like a roller coaster waiting at the top of a hill.
vocabulary.use_sentence: The car had a lot of potential energy before it started to roll down the big hill.

vocabulary.definition: The energy of motion — the faster something moves, the more kinetic energy it has.
vocabulary.use_sentence: As the coaster sped up, its kinetic energy increased.

vocabulary.definition: A force that happens when two surfaces rub together, slowing things down and creating heat.
vocabulary.use_sentence: Friction between the wheels and the track made the roller coaster slow down.

vocabulary.definition: Energy that transforms into heat because of friction — this energy leaves the motion system.
vocabulary.use_sentence: The heat loss from the brakes made them feel warm after the ride stopped.

vocabulary.definition: The scientific law that energy is never created or destroyed — it only changes form.
vocabulary.use_sentence: Because of energy conservation, the potential energy that was lost became kinetic energy and heat.

discussion_cards.prompt: Dr. Huang says friction is why coasters stop. Can you think of a way to reduce friction on a roller coaster track?
discussion_cards.frame: To reduce friction, an engineer could try to ___.

discussion_cards.prompt: If energy only changes form, describe the energy changes you see when you bounce a basketball.
Why is it important for an engineer like Dr. Huang to understand both potential and kinetic energy to build a safe ride?

reflection_frames.knew: Before reading, I already knew that ___
reflection_frames.learned: From the story, I learned that ___
